Off Calendar Jan-08-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAMES FARINARO, Esq. of Law Offices of James Farinaro (Tel: 510-740-9269) for Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon;
Atty. MARK A. HAGOPIAN, Esq. of Manning & Kass, Ellrod, Ramirez, Trester LLP (Tel: 415-217-6990) for Defendants Recology Inc., Golden Gate Disposal & Recycling Company, and John Borzoni.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS RECOLOGY, INC., GOLDEN GATE DISPOSAL & RECYCLING COMPANY, AND JOHN BORZONI's Motion for Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Recology, Inc., Golden Gate Disposal & Recycling Company and John Borzoni's summary judgment/adjudication motion is granted in part and denied in part. Plaintiff does not oppose summary judgment for Golden Gate or Borzoni, so it is granted. The first amended complaint (FAC) pleads causes of action against Recology for "negligence - motor vehicle" and "negligence - premises liability." At about 2 a.m. February 20, 2017, plaintiff got into a lidded dumpster to protect his computer from the rain. A garbage truck emptied the dumpster and compacted its load, seriously injuring plaintiff. He was later found at a trash collection site and spent three months in hospital. (PRDSS 8, 10; PSS 24-33.)

Recology demurred to the FAC. Judge Schulman's order overruling the demurrers repeatedly found a duty by "garbage collectors to rattle dumpsters before dumping the contents in their trucks." (Id. at 2:14-15, 2:26-28, 3:3-4.) In its pending motion, Recology equates the incidental "jostling" of a dumpster while it is being emptied with Judge Schulman's finding. That is not a fair reading of the order, which finds a duty to purposefully rattle dumpsters to alert anyone who may be inside. Moreover, the FAC pleads several other duties that Recology's motion does not address. (Id. at 7:19-25.) Recology's summary judgment/adjudication motion is denied because triable issues exist as to duties at a minimum.

Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Christine L. Jordan, CSR No. 12262, cjordan@sftc.org (302/RBU)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-08-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
James Farinaro, esq. (510-553-1200) for Plaintiff.
Mark A. Hagopian, esq. (415-400-0807) of Manning & Kass...LLP for Defendants, Recology, Inc., Golden Gate Disposal, and John Borzoni.
No other appearances.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SUE MILLS, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT:

Defendant Sue Mills, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend as to the second cause of action for premises liability. The potential risk of immersing oneself in a dumpster is obvious as a matter of law. (See Christoff v. Union Pacific Railroad Co. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 118, 126 [holding that railroad tracks provide a self-evident warning to an adult of ordinary intelligence "that it is not safe to walk upon it, or near enough to it to be struck by a passing train"]; Huffman v. City of Poway (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 975, 991 [holding that the obviousness of a condition is not a question of fact where "reasonable minds can come to only one conclusion"].) While Defendant may have a duty to take precautions to protect against risk of harm from an obviously unsafe condition, even if it does not have a duty to warn, it must be foreseeable that someone would encounter the condition. (See Osborn v. Mission Ready Mix (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 104, 121-22 ["[A]lthough the obviousness of a danger may obviate the duty to warn of its existence, if it is foreseeable that the danger may cause injury despite the fact that it is obvious (e.g., when necessity requires persons to encounter it), there may be a duty to remedy the danger, and the breach of that duty may in turn form the basis for liability, if the breach of duty was a proximate cause of any injury."] [emphasis in original].) The existence of such a duty turns on issues of foreseeability, the burden on the defendant, and the consequences to the community of imposing such a duty. (Id. at 121.)

Finding a duty would not require Sue Mills to abandon trash service, but it would impose burdens by requiring businesses to obtain locks, make signs, or check dumpsters for people on trash day. Moreover, while the FAC alleges that the business is located in an impoverished neighborhood with homeless people who may have reasons to search in dumpsters for shelter, food, or other supplies, it does not allege that an accident such as this has occurred on these premises before. (FAC Par. 24.) The trash collection defendants are better situated to warn individuals in dumpsters before the contents are dumped into their trucks than businesses who merely house dumpsters on their properties. Finally, Plaintiff does not allege that he was foreseeably required to immerse himself in the dumpster, thus subjecting himself to the dangers imposed by the obviously dangerous condition. (See Jacobs v. Coldwell (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 438, 447 [no duty because there was no evidence that plaintiff was "foreseeably required to expose himself to the danger of falling into the empty pool"].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR#6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-08-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
James Farinaro, esq. (510-553-1200) for Plaintiff.
Mark A. Hagopian, esq. (415-400-0807) of Manning & Kass...LLP for Defendants, Recology, Inc., Golden Gate Disposal, and John Borzoni.
No other appearances.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs RECOLOGY, INC., GOLDEN GATE DISPOSAL & RECYCLING COMPANY, and JOHN BORZONI's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT of Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon:

Defendants Recology, Inc., Golden Gate Disposal & Recycling Company, and John Borzoni's demurrer to Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon's first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled as to both causes of action for motor vehicle negligence and premises liability. "The existence of a duty of care toward an interest of another worthy of legal protection is the essential prerequisite to a negligence cause of action, determined as a matter of law by the court." (Software Design & Application, Ltd. v. Hoefer & Arnett, Inc. (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 472, 478.) While the Court's April 15, 2019 order concluded that garbage collection companies do not have a duty of care to warn individuals of the risks of secreting themselves in dumpsters, that order was limited to an analysis of the facts alleged in the initial complaint. The FAC adds new allegations that are sufficient to give rise to such a duty. (FAC Paras. 11-16, 24-28.)

In light of the new facts alleged in the FAC, an analysis of the Rowland factors laid out in Cabral v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2011) 51 Cal.4th 764, 771 [citing Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108] yields the conclusion that the injury to Plaintiff was foreseeable and that it would not be unreasonably burdensome to impose a duty on garbage collectors to rattle dumpsters before dumping the contents into their trucks. "The most important factor to consider in determining whether to create an exception to the general duty to exercise ordinary care articulated by section 1714 is whether the injury in question was foreseeable." (Kesner v. Superior Court (2016) 1 Cal.5th 1132, 1145.) The FAC pleads that Defendants were aware of prior instances of garbage collectors encountering persons inside of dumpsters, including one specific incident on July 22, 2014, which preceded the accident that gave rise to this case. (FAC Paras. 12-14.) The FAC also alleges that Recology and Golden Gate advise garbage truck drivers of the risks of homeless people seeking shelter in dumpsters and that they should "rattle the bins" before emptying them. (FAC Para. 15.) These allegations, combined with more specific allegations that homeless people enter dumpsters for shelter, recyclables, and food, are enough to conclude that the injury to Plaintiff was foreseeable. (FAC Para. 11.)

While the facts alleged suggest only minimal moral blame, they also show that the costs and burdens on Defendants to rattle dumpsters or otherwise warn hidden individuals of the imminent dumping of the trash are minimal. (FAC Para. 16.) Therefore, as pled, the FAC presents facts that distinguish this case from other cases where societal burdens of imposing a duty were much greater. (See Cabral, 51 Cal.4th at 772 [citing cases in which courts declined to find a tort duty in light of the societal burdens that recognizing such a duty would create].) A duty on the part of drivers to rattle a dumpster is not unduly burdensome.

Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR#6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
